deviations.gdpr_quote: confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed
deviations.policy_quote: whether and which personal data we store about you.
deviations.mismatch: The policy narrows the Art 15(1) right from all processing to only what is stored.
deviations.analysis: Limiting the confirmation to stored data excludes processing operations beyond storage, narrowing the GDPR’s broader 'are being processed' scope.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed, and, where that is the case, access to the personal data and the following information:
deviations.policy_quote: The right to information does not exist or is subject to limitations if and to the extent that confidential information, such as information that is subject to professional secrecy, would be disclosed.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR Art 15(1) grants a right of access/information, while the policy declares that this right does not exist or is limited when confidential/professionally secret information would be disclosed.
deviations.analysis: Within the scope of Art 15(1) and its cross-reference to Art 22(1), no such limitation is stated. The policy introduces an additional condition that negates or limits the right beyond what is expressed in Art 15(1).

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed
deviations.policy_quote: Right of access: You can request information from us at any time about whether and which personal data we store about you.
deviations.mismatch: The policy limits access to personal data that are "stored" rather than all personal data "being processed" as required by GDPR.
deviations.analysis: Art15(1) covers any personal data being processed (not limited to storage). The policy narrows the scope to stored data only.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller confirmation as to whether or not personal data concerning him or her are being processed, and, where that is the case, access to the personal data and the following information:
deviations.policy_quote: The right to information does not exist or is subject to limitations if and to the extent that confidential information, such as information that is subject to professional secrecy, would be disclosed.
deviations.mismatch: The policy introduces a limitation on the Art15(1) access right based on confidentiality/professional secrecy that is not stated in the cited GDPR provision.
deviations.analysis: Within the provided Art15(1) text, the right of access is stated without the professional-secrecy limitation the policy adds, thereby narrowing the right’s scope.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6(1), or point (a) of Article 9(2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing;
deviations.policy_quote: if the processing is based on your consent, you have withdrawn your consent.
deviations.mismatch: The policy grants erasure upon any withdrawal of consent without the GDPR’s qualifier that there must be no other legal ground for the processing.
deviations.analysis: By omitting the condition “and where there is no other legal ground,” the policy is more generous than GDPR, expanding the circumstances under which erasure is promised.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the controller shall have the obligation to erase personal data without undue delay
deviations.policy_quote: we must stop processing your personal data and remove it from our IT systems and databases.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR requires erasure of personal data generally, while the policy confines deletion to 'IT systems and databases,' narrowing the medium/scope.
deviations.analysis: Specifying only IT systems and databases potentially excludes other media or locations of personal data, narrowing the scope relative to the GDPR’s medium-neutral erasure obligation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the data subject withdraws consent on which the processing is based according to point (a) of Article 6(1), or point (a) of Article 9(2), and where there is no other legal ground for the processing;
deviations.policy_quote: if the processing is based on your consent, you have withdrawn your consent.
deviations.mismatch: The policy grants erasure upon consent withdrawal without the GDPR caveat that there must be no other legal ground for the processing.
deviations.analysis: Article 17(1)(b) conditions erasure on both consent withdrawal and the absence of any other legal basis; the policy omits the latter, over-committing to erase.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller the erasure of personal data concerning him or her without undue delay ... where one of the following grounds applies: (a) the personal data are no longer necessary ...; (b) the data subject withdraws consent ... and where there is no other legal ground for the processing; (c) the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(1) and there are no overriding legitimate grounds ... or the data subject objects to the processing pursuant to Article 21(2); (d) the personal data have been unlawfully processed; (e) the personal data have to be erased for compliance with a legal obligation ...; (f) the personal data have been collected in relation to the offer of information society services referred to in Article 8(1).
deviations.policy_quote: Right to erasure: You have the right to request that we erase your personal data if and to the extent that the data is no longer needed for the purposes for which it was collected or, if the processing is based on your consent, you have withdrawn your consent.
deviations.mismatch: The policy limits erasure to two grounds (no longer needed; consent withdrawn) and omits other GDPR grounds such as objection under Art 21(1)/(2), unlawful processing, legal obligation to erase, and ISS to a child.
deviations.analysis: The phrase 'if and to the extent that ... or ...' reads as limiting rather than illustrative. This narrows the scope of erasure grounds compared to Art 17(1) and its cross-references (Art 6(1), Art 9(2), Art 21(1)/(2), Art 8(1)).

deviations.gdpr_quote: Paragraphs 1 and 2 shall not apply to the extent that processing is necessary: (a) for exercising the right of freedom of expression and information; (b) for compliance with a legal obligation ... or for the performance of a task carried out in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority vested in the controller; (c) for reasons of public interest in the area of public health in accordance with points (h) and (i) of Article 9(2) as well as Article 9(3); (d) for archiving purposes in the public interest, scientific or historical research purposes or statistical purposes in accordance with Article 89(1) ...; or (e) for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims.
deviations.policy_quote: A right to erasure does not exist insofar as • the data may not be deleted due to a legal obligation or must be processed due to a legal obligation; • the data processing is necessary for the assertion, exercise or defence of legal claims.
deviations.mismatch: The policy lists only legal obligation and legal claims as exceptions, omitting freedom of expression, public interest in public health, and archiving/research/statistics exceptions allowed by GDPR.
deviations.analysis: By stating only two exceptions, the policy is stricter (over-compliant) than GDPR, effectively expanding the erasure right beyond what GDPR requires. References to Art 9(2)(h),(i), Art 9(3), and Art 89(1) substantiate the missing exceptions.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies: (a) the accuracy of the personal data is contested by the data subject, for a period enabling the controller to verify the accuracy of the personal data; (b) the processing is unlawful and the data subject opposes the erasure of the personal data and requests the restriction of their use instead; (c) the controller no longer needs the personal data for the purposes of the processing, but they are required by the data subject for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims; (d) the data subject has objected to processing pursuant to Article 21(1) pending the verification whether the legitimate grounds of the controller override those of the data subject.
deviations.policy_quote: Right to restrict processing: You have the right to request that we restrict the processing of your personal data.
deviations.mismatch: The policy frames restriction as an unconditional right, whereas GDPR limits it to specific conditions in points (a)–(d).
deviations.analysis: By not limiting the right to the enumerated grounds, the policy over-commits beyond GDPR’s conditional entitlement.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to obtain from the controller restriction of processing where one of the following applies:
deviations.policy_quote: Right to restrict processing: You have the right to request that we restrict the processing of your personal data.
deviations.mismatch: GDPR grants a right to obtain restriction when conditions apply, while the policy frames it as a right only to request restriction.
deviations.analysis: The wording downgrades a guaranteed entitlement ('obtain') to a mere request, potentially implying discretionary acceptance by the controller. Conditions (a)-(d) are omitted but omissions are treated as Non-Deviation.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the data subject shall have the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible.
deviations.policy_quote: as well as the right to have this data transferred to another controller.
deviations.mismatch: The policy omits the GDPR’s “where technically feasible” limitation, over-committing to perform transfers in all cases.
deviations.analysis: By not conditioning the transfer on technical feasibility, the policy is stricter than GDPR’s minimum requirement.

deviations.gdpr_quote: the right to have the personal data transmitted directly from one controller to another, where technically feasible.
deviations.policy_quote: as well as the right to have this data transferred to another controller.
deviations.mismatch: The policy drops the “directly from one controller to another” modality prescribed by GDPR Art20(2).
deviations.analysis: GDPR Art20(2) is about direct controller-to-controller transmission; the policy’s phrasing is method-agnostic and may imply indirect transfer.

deviations.gdpr_quote: The data subject shall have the right to object, on grounds relating to his or her particular situation, at any time to processing of personal data concerning him or her which is based on point (e) or (f) of Article 6(1)
deviations.policy_quote: Right to object to processing: If the processing of your data is based on Art 6 para 1 lit f) GDPR, you may object to the processing at any time.
deviations.mismatch: Policy allows unconditional objection to processing based on Art 6(1)(f), whereas GDPR requires that the objection be based on grounds relating to the data subject’s particular situation.
deviations.analysis: By omitting the ‘particular situation’ condition, the policy broadens the right to object beyond the GDPR’s requirement. Non-mention of Art 6(1)(e), profiling, or the balancing exception are treated as omissions, not deviations.

deviations.gdpr_quote: A transfer of personal data to a third country or an international organisation may take place where the Commission has decided that the third country, a territory or one or more specified sectors within that third country, or the international organisation in question ensures an adequate level of protection. Such a transfer shall not require any specific authorisation.
deviations.policy_quote: For data transfers within the PwC network, the PwC network companies have, among other things, concluded an internal data protection agreement which provides for compliance with the EU standard contractual clauses of the EU Commission within the meaning of Art 46 para 2 lit c) GDPR for the transfer of personal data from EU/EEA countries to PwC network companies outside the EU/EEA.
deviations.mismatch: The policy commits to applying SCCs for intra-network third-country transfers even where GDPR allows adequacy-based transfers without additional safeguards or authorisation.
deviations.analysis: Art 45(1) permits transfers to adequate countries without further safeguards; committing to SCCs for all intra-network third-country transfers imposes a stricter requirement than necessary (over-compliance).

deviations.gdpr_quote: every data subject shall have the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority, in particular in the Member State of his or her habitual residence, place of work or place of the alleged infringement if the data subject considers that the processing of personal data relating to him or her infringes this Regulation.
deviations.policy_quote: In accordance with Art 77 GDPR, you have the right to lodge a complaint with a data protection supervisory authority if you believe that the processing of your personal data infringes data protection law.
deviations.mismatch: The policy expands the trigger from infringements of “this Regulation” to infringements of broader “data protection law.”
deviations.analysis: GDPR Art 77(1) specifies the complaint right applies where processing infringes the GDPR itself; the policy broadens this condition to any data protection law, altering the scope of when a complaint may be lodged.

deviations.gdpr_quote: if the data subject considers that the processing of personal data relating to him or her infringes this Regulation.
deviations.policy_quote: In accordance with Art 77 GDPR, you have the right to lodge a complaint with a data protection supervisory authority if you believe that the processing of your personal data infringes data protection law.
deviations.mismatch: Policy expands the complaint trigger from infringement of “this Regulation” to any “data protection law,” over-broadening the basis for complaint.
deviations.analysis: GDPR ties the complaint right in Art 77(1) specifically to infringements of the GDPR, whereas the policy grants the right for broader ‘data protection law’ infringements, which is more permissive toward data subjects.
